message:
feat(upgrade): verify SHA256 of downloaded release archive before replacing binary

Previously `br upgrade` delegated entirely to `self_update::update()`,
which downloads the release archive over HTTPS, extracts it, and
atomically replaces the running binary — but performs no integrity
check against the release manifest. A compromised CDN, a man-in-the
middle with a valid TLS cert, or even a truncated transfer would be
accepted as long as the archive was a well-formed tar.gz. Given that
the release manifest publishes per-archive `.sha256` sidecar assets,
the upgrade path should refuse to install an archive whose hash
doesn't match.

Introduce a direct download+verify+extract+move pipeline replacing the
upstream `.update()` call, using the public building blocks the
`self_update` 0.44 crate exposes (`Download`, `Extract`, `Move`,
`self_replace`). The upstream API has zipsign signing support but no
SHA256 verification hook, so we handle asset selection ourselves.

New helpers in `src/cli/commands/upgrade.rs`:
- `is_archive_asset_name(name)` — true for `*.tar.gz` / `*.zip` that
  aren't themselves `.sha256` or `.minisig` sidecars.
- `release_binary_asset_for(release, target, identifier)` — primary
  match by target+identifier, falls back to identifier-only (or any
  archive). Replaces the old `assets.first()` heuristic that could
  grab the checksum file.
- `checksum_asset_for(release, archive_name)` — exact-name lookup
  for `<archive_name>.sha256`.
- `select_release_for_update(updater, current_version)` — honors
  `updater.target_version()` if set; otherwise picks the first
  version-bump-compatible release from `get_latest_releases`, falling
  back to the latest release so users don't get stuck when
  bump-is-compatible returns nothing (same semantics the upstream
  crate uses internally).
- `update_with_checksum(updater, release)` — full pipeline:
    1. Find the target-matching archive asset and its paired
       `.sha256`. Fail loudly if either is missing.
    2. Download the archive to a TempDir via
       `download_asset_to_writer` (which sets `Accept:
       application/octet-stream` and `Authorization: token ...`
       when a token is configured, matching upstream behaviour and
       enabling private releases to work).
    3. Download the checksum asset, parse it with
       `parse_sha256_checksum` (tolerates sha256sum-format lines,
       requires exact archive-name match when the line names a file,
       rejects lines whose hash isn't 64 hex characters).
    4. Stream-hash the downloaded archive in 8 KiB chunks via
       `sha256_file`, compare case-insensitively to the expected
       value, error out with both hashes in the message on mismatch.
    5. Extract the expected bin path, and either self-replace (when
       the install target matches the running executable) or
       `Move::from_source.to_dest(...)` for out-of-tree installs.
- Rewire the two metadata call sites (`execute_check` and
  `execute_dry_run`) to use `release_binary_asset_for` so the
  reported download URL matches what would actually be installed.
- `execute_upgrade` now calls `select_release_for_update` first and
  short-circuits when there's nothing newer (JSON and rich paths
  both get the "already up to date" variants), then hands the
  selected release to `update_with_checksum`. Preserves the
  existing "archive-tar missing" error hint.

Unit tests:
- `release_binary_asset_ignores_checksum_and_signature_assets` —
  among three candidates (`.tar.gz.sha256`, `.tar.gz.minisig`,
  `.tar.gz`), picks the raw archive.
- `checksum_asset_matches_archive_name_exactly` — among multiple
  `.sha256` siblings, picks the one matching the resolved archive's
  exact name.
- `parse_sha256_checksum_accepts_standard_sha256sum_line` — the
  canonical `<hash>  <filename>` form returns a lowercased hex hash.
- `parse_sha256_checksum_rejects_wrong_archive_name` — a line that
  names a different archive is ignored and the parse error surfaces
  the expected filename.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
